definitions.definition: to make something less clear and harder to understand, especially intentionally
examples: She was criticized for using arguments that obfuscated the main issue.
Companies deliberately obfuscate figures in complicated annual reports.
Instead of concealing or obfuscating, the doctors involved admitted their fault and launched an investigation.

definitions.definition: to put false or confusing information on the internet, especially using special software, in order to stop search engines being able to collect information about you
examples: You can confuse web trackers by obfuscating your movements online.
The browser extension is designed to obfuscate browsing data and protect users from tracking by advertising networks.

definitions.definition: to create computer code (= instructions used to program computers) that is difficult for humans to understand, in order to reduce the code size and hide it from other people
examples: Much source code encryption software obfuscates the code.

examples: He spent €180 on a hat.
I've wasted a lot of money on this car.
We made a big profit on that deal.
How much interest are you paying on the loan?

definitions.definition: used to show a person or thing that is necessary for something to happen or that is the origin of something
examples: We're relying on you.
I might come - it depends on Andrew.
Most children remain dependent on their parents while they are at university.
His latest movie is based on a fairy tale.

definitions.definition: used to show when someone is involved or taking part in something
examples: I'm working on a new book.
"Where had we got up to?" "We were on page 42."

definitions.definition: used to show what is providing financial support or an income
examples: I only have $70 a week to live on at the moment.
He retired on a generous pension from the company.
She's on (= earning) £25,000 a year.

definitions.definition: used to show something that is used as food, fuel, or a drug
examples: What do mice live on?
Does this radio run on batteries?
Is he on drugs?

definitions.definition: next to or along the side of
examples: It's a small town on the Mississippi River.
Our house was on Sturton Street.
Strasbourg is on the border of France and Germany.

definitions.definition: used to show when someone is a member of a group or organization
examples: Have you ever served on a jury?
There are no women on the committee.
How many people are on your staff?
She's a researcher on a women's magazine.

definitions.definition: used when referring to a tool, instrument, or system that is used to do something
examples: I do all my household accounts on the computer.
Chris is on drums and Mike's on bass guitar.
I'm on (= talking on) the phone.

definitions.definition: used to show when something is repeated one or more times
examples: The government suffered defeat on defeat in the local elections.
Wave on wave of refugees has crossed the border to escape the fighting.

definitions.definition: used when making a comparison
examples: That's my final offer, and I can't improve on it.
The manufacturer has never improved on the earliest model of the car.
The productivity figures are down/up on last week's.

definitions.definition: used to show when someone has something in a pocket or in a bag that they are carrying
examples: Do you have a spare pen on you?
I don't have my driving licence on me.

definitions.definition: happening after and usually because of
examples: Acting on information given to them anonymously, the police arrested him.
He inherited a quarter of a million pounds on his mother's death.
On their return they discovered that their house had been broken into.

definitions.definition: used to show who is paying for something
examples: Dinner meal is on me.
She had her operation done on the National Health Service.

definitions.definition: used to show who suffers when something does not operate as it should
examples: The phone suddenly went dead on me.
Their car broke down on them on the way home.

definitions.definition: used to show the number of points a person or team has in a competition
examples: Clive's team is on five points while Joan's is on seven.

definitions.definition: on your body or someone's body
examples: It's very cold so put a coat on.
She wanders around the house with nothing on.
Can you remember what he had on (= was wearing)?
I tried on a few jackets, but none of them looked nice.

definitions.definition: covering the surface of something or connected to something
examples: Screw the lid on tightly.
Surgeons managed to sew the finger back on.

definitions.definition: used to show when something is operating or starting to operate
examples: Could you switch on the radio?
Would you turn the TV on?
You left the bedroom light on.

definitions.definition: continuing or not stopping
examples: If her phone's busy, keep on trying.
Stop talking and get on with your work.
If Elise would just hang on (= wait) a little longer she'd definitely get the promotion.
The noise just went on and on (= continued for a long time), and I thought it would never stop.

definitions.definition: into a bus, train, plane, etc., or in the correct position to start using some other method of travelling
examples: The train suddenly started moving as I was getting on.
Her horse galloped off as soon as she was on.

definitions.definition: performing
examples: Hurry up with the make-up - I'm on in ten minutes.
The audience cheered as the band came on (= came onto the stage).

definitions.definition: continuing forward in time or space
examples: They never spoke to each other from that day on (= after that day).
What are you doing later on?
When you're done with it, would you pass it on to Paul?
Move on, please, and let the ambulance through.
You cycle on and I'll meet you there.

definitions.definition: happening or planned
examples: Is the party still on for tomorrow?
I'm busy tomorrow, but I've got nothing on the day after.
I've got a lot on at the moment.
Food had to be rationed when the war was on.
Are there any good movies on (= being shown) this week?

definitions.definition: used when talking about the position of one thing compared with the position of another
examples: It's amazing nobody was injured because the two buses crashed head on (= the front parts of the buses hit each other).
The bike hit our car side on (= hit the side of the car rather than the front or back).
It would be easier to get the bookcase through the doorway if we turned it sideways on (= turned it so that one of its sides is at the front).

definitions.definition: used to refer to things that are carried by a vehicle and form part of it
examples: The car comes with an on-board satellite navigation device.

definitions.definition: one single time
examples: I went sailing once, but I didn't like it.
We have lunch together once a month.

definitions.definition: at the same time
examples: They all started talking at once.

definitions.definition: used when something happens that does not usually happen
examples: For once, the bus came on time.

definitions.definition: used to say that you will only do or request something on this particular occasion
examples: All right, I'll lend you the money - just this once.

definitions.definition: again, as has happened before
examples: Once again, racist attacks are increasing across Europe.

definitions.definition: one more time
examples: I'd like to visit the campus once more before we leave.

definitions.definition: again, as has happened before
examples: When my father came home, everything felt right once more.

definitions.definition: a few times
examples: I've seen him once or twice in town.

definitions.definition: sometimes but not often
examples: We meet for lunch once in a while.

definitions.definition: completely and in a way that will finally solve a problem
examples: Our intention is to destroy their offensive capability once and for all.

definitions.definition: only likely to happen once in a person's life
examples: An opportunity as good as this arises once in a lifetime.

definitions.definition: on a single occasion
examples: I've only played rugby the once, and I never want to play it again.

definitions.definition: in the past, but not now
examples: This house once belonged to my grandfather.
Computers are much cheaper nowadays than they once were.
Once-thriving villages stand deserted and in ruins.

definitions.definition: as soon as, or from the moment when
examples: Once I've found somewhere to live I'll send you my address.
Remember that you won't be able to cancel the contract once you've signed.

definitions.definition: immediately
examples: I knew at once that I'd like it here.

definitions.definition: a gene that is present in every cell and causes a healthy cell to become cancerous under particular conditions
a gene that is present in every cell and causes a healthy cell to become cancerous under particular conditions
related to the study and treatment of tumours (= masses of cells, as in cancer) in the body
examples: He specializes in oncological medicine.
oncological treatment

definitions.definition: a doctor who studies and treats tumours (= masses of cells, as in cancer) in the body
examples: His oncologist suggested that he join the clinical trial.
The surgeon fixed an appointment with a leading oncologist.

definitions.definition: a doctor who studies and treats tumours (= masses of cells, as in cancer) in the body
examples: His oncologist suggested that he join the clinical trial.
The surgeon fixed an appointment with a leading oncologist.

definitions.definition: the study and treatment of tumours (= masses of cells) in the body
moving towards you or coming nearer
examples: The car veered onto the wrong side of the road and hit an oncoming truck.
There seemed to be no way of averting the oncoming crisis.

definitions.definition: a cost that an employer has when they employ someone, in addition to the cost of paying the person's salary or wages
examples: On-costs include pension contributions and payroll tax.

definitions.definition: like a dream, or relating to dreams
examples: The film thrills with its indelible, oneiric imagery.
These two dreams illustrate two possible points of view for the same oneiric phenomenon.

definitions.definition: a state of being joined as one thing, and no longer separate
examples: a sense of oneness with the moment

definitions.definition: the fact of being one single thing
examples: the oneness of God

definitions.definition: difficult to do or needing a lot of effort
examples: the onerous task of finding a peaceful solution
She found the duties of motherhood onerous.

definitions.definition: the quality of being very difficult or needing a lot of effort
examples: I had no idea of the onerousness of the journey that was to follow.
They complained about the onerousness and the difficulty of routine work.

definitions.definition: the number 1
examples: He had two slices and I only had one.
She'll be one year old tomorrow.

definitions.definition: a member of a group of people or things
examples: One of their daughters has just had a baby.
EMI is one of the world's largest record companies.
Finding a cure for cancer is one of the biggest challenges facing medical researchers.
Our organization is just one of many charities that are providing famine relief in the region.

definitions.definition: used to refer to a time in the future that is not yet decided
examples: Why don't we meet for lunch one day next week?
I'd like to go skiing one Christmas.

definitions.definition: used to refer to a particular occasion while avoiding stating the exact moment
examples: One night we stayed up talking till dawn.
He was attacked as he was walking home from work late one afternoon.
One moment he says he loves me, the next moment he's asking for a divorce.
She never seems to know what she's doing from one minute to the next.

definitions.definition: a single thing; not two or more
examples: Eat them one at a time (= separately).
I think we should paint the bedroom all one (= in a single) colour.
Do you think five of us will manage to squeeze into the one car?

definitions.definition: combined in a single person or object
examples: With this model you get a radio, CD player, and MP3 dock all in one.

definitions.definition: used when saying there is no other person or thing
examples: He's the one person you can rely on in an emergency.
This may be your one and only (= only ever) opportunity to meet her.
My final guest on tonight's show needs no introduction. Please welcome the one and only Michael Jordan!

definitions.definition: used before the name of someone who is not known
examples: Her solicitor is one John Wintersgill.

definitions.definition: used to emphasize an adjective
examples: His mother is one (= a very) generous woman.
That's one (= a very) big ice cream cone you have there.
It was one hell of a (= a very great) shock to find out I'd lost my job.

definitions.definition: used to refer to a particular thing or person within a group or range of things or people that are possible or available
examples: I have a few books on Chinese food. You can borrow one if you want.
Which one would you like?
Please make a copy for everybody in the office and a few extra ones for the visitors.
"Which cake would you like?" "The one in the front."
French croissants are so much better than the ones we get here.
There were lots of people watching, and not one of them offered to help.
I've received no replies to my job applications - not a single one (= none).
Chris is the one (= the person) with curly brown hair.

definitions.definition: to never do something
examples: I'm not one to criticize other people, as you know.

definitions.definition: to like something very much
examples: I've never been one for staying out late.
He's a great one for the ladies.

definitions.definition: used to talk about one person or thing compared with other similar or related people or things
examples: They look so similar it's often difficult to distinguish one from the other.
You may have one or the other, but not both.
Crime and freedom are inseparable. You can't have one without the other.

definitions.definition: any person, but not a particular person
examples: One has an obligation to one's friends.
One ought to make the effort to vote.

definitions.definition: the person speaking or writing
examples: Of course, one (= I) would be delighted to dine with the Queen.

definitions.definition: the reflexive form of the pronoun 'one' when it refers to people in general or to the person speaking
examples: One has to learn to control oneself.

definitions.definition: continuing to exist or develop, or happening at the present moment
examples: an ongoing investigation/process/project
No agreement has yet been reached and the negotiations are still ongoing.

definitions.definition: a vegetable with a strong smell and flavour, made up of several layers surrounding each other tightly in a round shape, usually brown or red on the outside and white inside
examples: I always cry when I'm chopping onions.
Fry the onion and garlic for about two minutes.

definitions.definition: a vegetable with a strong smell and flavour, made up of several layers surrounding each other tightly in a round shape, usually brown or red on the outside and white inside
examples: I always cry when I'm chopping onions.
Fry the onion and garlic for about two minutes.

definitions.definition: Online products, services, or information can be bought or used on the internet
examples: an online newspaper/magazine/dictionary
online banking/shopping

definitions.definition: bought, used, etc. using the internet
examples: Do you like to shop online?
This dictionary went online in 1999.

definitions.definition: to be able to use email or the internet
examples: I'll send you my email address once I'm online.

definitions.definition: connected to a system
examples: The new power station is expected to be online by July.
When will the new factory come online (= start production)?

definitions.definition: someone who watches something that is happening in a public place but is not involved in it
examples: A crowd of curious onlookers soon gathered to see what was happening.

definitions.definition: someone who watches something that is happening in a public place but is not involved in it
examples: A crowd of curious onlookers soon gathered to see what was happening.

definitions.definition: used to show that there is a single one or very few of something, or that there are no others
examples: I was the only person on the train.
Is this really the only way to do it?
The only thing that matters is that the baby is healthy.
It was the only thing I could do under the circumstances.
Rita was the only person to complain.

definitions.definition: used to show that something is limited to not more than, or is not anything other than, the people, things, amount, or activity stated
examples: At present these televisions are only available in Japan.
Only Sue and Mark bothered to turn up for the meeting.
This club is for members only.
Only an idiot would do that.
These shoes only cost $50.
Don't worry - it's only a scratch.
I was only joking.
I was only trying to help.
I only arrived half an hour ago.
She spoke to me only a few minutes ago on the phone.
It's only four o'clock and it's already getting dark.
"Who's there?" "It's only me (= it is not someone you should worry about). I've locked myself out."
It's only natural that you should worry about your children.

definitions.definition: used to refer to something that happens almost immediately after something else
examples: People were leaving and I'd only just arrived.
We'd only just set off when the car broke down.

definitions.definition: almost not
examples: There was only just enough food to go round.
We got there in time for our flight, but only just (= but we almost did not).

definitions.definition: used to say that two related things are true or happened, especially when this is surprising or shocking
examples: Not only did he turn up late, he also forgot his books.
If this project fails, it will affect not only our department, but also the whole organization.

definitions.definition: If you say you have only (got) to do something, you mean that it is all you need to do in order to achieve something else
examples: If you want any help, you have only to ask.
You only have to look at her face to see that she's not well.

definitions.definition: used when saying that something unpleasant will happen as a result of an action or a failure to act
examples: If you don't do something about it now it will only get worse.

definitions.definition: used to show that you feel sorry about something that cannot happen when explaining why it cannot happen
examples: I'd love to go to Australia. I only wish I could afford to.

definitions.definition: used to emphasize what you are hoping or wishing for
examples: I only hope you know what you're doing.
I only wish that they would keep in touch more regularly.

definitions.definition: used to show that you think someone has done something silly
examples: She's only locked herself out of her flat again!

definitions.definition: used to show what is the single or main reason why something mentioned in the first part of the sentence cannot be performed or is not completely true
examples: I'd invite Frances to the party, only (= but I will not because) I don't want her husband to come.
I'd call him myself, only (= but I cannot because) I'm at work all day.
I'd be happy to do it for you, only (= but) don't expect it to be done before next week.
This fabric is similar to wool, only (= except that it is) cheaper.

definitions.definition: written abbreviation for or near(est) offerused in advertisements for things that people are trying to sell to show that they will accept slightly less money than the price they are asking for
examples: Ladies' bike - excellent condition. £80 o.n.o.

definitions.definition: the act of creating or using words that include sounds that are similar to the noises the words refer to
(of a word) sounding similar to the noise the word refers to
examples: "Pop", "boom", and "squelch" are onomatopoeic words.
The word "click" is onomatopoeic in itself.

definitions.definition: abbreviation for the Office for National Statisticsa UK government department that collects information about the country's society and economy
examples: The ONS said that unemployment rose by 2% last month.

definitions.definition: appearing on a computer screen or a television screen
examples: Insert the disk and then follow the onscreen prompts to restore your system.
Select which programme you want to record from the onscreen TV guide.

definitions.definition: on a computer screen or on a television screen
examples: Most editing is now done onscreen.

definitions.definition: the moment at which something unpleasant begins
examples: the onset of winter
The new treatment can delay the onset of the disease by several years.

definitions.definition: the first part of a syllable
(of an illness or medical condition) starting at the stated time or in the stated way
examples: early-onset dementia
sudden-onset hearing loss

definitions.definition: the first part of a syllable
(of an illness or medical condition) starting at the stated time or in the stated way
examples: early-onset dementia
sudden-onset hearing loss

definitions.definition: moving towards land from the sea, or on land rather than at sea
examples: onshore winds
onshore oil reserves

definitions.definition: (in football and some other sports) in a position where you are allowed to kick, throw, or receive the ball or puck
a very powerful attack
examples: It is unlikely that his forces could withstand an enemy onslaught for very long.
Scotland's onslaught on Wales in the second half of the match earned them a 4–1 victory.

definitions.definition: a very powerful attack
examples: It is unlikely that his forces could withstand an enemy onslaught for very long.
Scotland's onslaught on Wales in the second half of the match earned them a 4–1 victory.

definitions.definition: onto or on a stage for a performance
examples: The audience cheered as the band walked onstage for another encore.
onstage dancers

definitions.definition: used to show movement into or on a particular place
examples: I slipped as I stepped onto the platform.
The sheep were loaded onto trucks.

definitions.definition: used about changing to, or starting to talk about, a different subject
examples: How did we get onto this subject?
Can we move onto the next item on the agenda?

definitions.definition: to keep holding something
examples: Hold onto my hand and you'll be perfectly safe.

definitions.definition: knowing about someone or something that can be useful to you
examples: You're onto a good thing with this buy-one-get-one-free deal.
David put me onto (= told me about) a really good restaurant.

definitions.definition: knowing about something bad someone has done
examples: He knows we're onto him.
Who put the police onto (= told the police about) her?

definitions.definition: If you are onto someone, you talk to that person, especially to ask them to do something, or to complain to them
examples: I must get onto the plumber about the shower.
Dad was onto her again about doing her homework.

definitions.definition: used about someone or something that is added to or joins a particular thing
examples: Imir's been voted onto the union committee.
I've been having problems loading this software onto my computer.

definitions.definition: relating to ontology
examples: the ontological argument for the existence of God

definitions.definition: the part of philosophy that studies what it means to exist
the responsibility or duty to do something
examples: The onus is on the landlord to ensure that the property is habitable.
We are trying to shift the onus for passenger safety onto the government.

definitions.definition: moving forward to a later time or a more distant (= farther away) place
examples: the onward march of time
If you are continuing on an onward flight, your bags will be transferred automatically.

definitions.definition: → onwards
beginning at a particular time and continuing after it
examples: I'm usually at home from five o'clock onwards.

definitions.definition: If you move onwards, you continue to go forwards
examples: We sailed onwards in a westerly direction.

definitions.definition: a valuable stone that is used in jewellery, with white, black, grey, and other coloured stripes
a very large amount of something pleasant
examples: She inherited oodles of money from her uncle.

definitions.definition: an expression of surprise, pleasure, approval, disapproval, or pain
examples: Ooh, what a gorgeous dress!
Ooh, yes, that would be nice!
Ooh, that's really mean!

definitions.definition: the study or collecting of eggs, especially birds' eggs
a type of Chinese tea with curly leaves
examples: Brew oolong and black teas at 180 to 200 degrees.

definitions.definition: power, strength, or energetic activity
examples: It's important to have a person with some oomph in charge of the department.
You want a car with a bit of oomph.

definitions.definition: an expression of surprise or feeling sorry about a mistake or slight accident
examples: Oops! I typed two Ls by mistake.

examples: Blood was still oozing out of the wound.
She removed the bandage to reveal a red swollen wound oozing pus.
The waiter brought her a massive pizza oozing (with) cheese.
He oozes (= has a lot of) charm/confidence.

definitions.definition: a thick brown liquid made of earth and water, found at the bottom of a river or lake
examples: Many millions of years ago, our ancestors climbed out of the primeval ooze onto dry land.

examples: Blood was still oozing out of the wound.

He oozes (= has a lot of) charm/confidence.

definitions.definition: a medical operation
examples: How long did you take to recover from your op?

definitions.definition: an opportunity
examples: a photo op (= a chance for a politician, etc. to be photographed looking good or doing good things)

definitions.definition: written abbreviation for opus
examples: Dvorak's Piano Concerto in G Minor, Op. 33

definitions.definition: abbreviation for original posterused on the internet to refer to the person who first started a conversation about something
examples: I just can't relate to the OP's comment.
I would agree that he is harassing the OP.

definitions.definition: the state of being opaque, or the degree to which something is opaque
a precious stone whose colour changes when the position of the person looking at it changes
the quality of reflecting light and changing colour like an opal
examples: the milky opalescence of the northern sea
The hedge rose against the pale opalescence of the lower sky.

definitions.definition: Something that is opalescent reflects light and changes colour like an opal
examples: the opalescent scales of a fish

definitions.definition: a precious stone whose colour changes when the position of the person looking at it changes
preventing light from travelling through, and therefore not transparent or translucent
examples: opaque glass/tights

definitions.definition: Opaque writing or speech is difficult to understand
examples: I find her poetry a little too opaque.

definitions.definition: in a way that is difficult to understand
examples: Her eyes were opaquely sad.
"I would be honoured," he said, while smiling opaquely.

definitions.definition: not closed or fastened
examples: an open door/window
An open suitcase lay on her bed.
You left the container open.
Someone had left the window wide (= completely) open.
He had several nasty open wounds (= those which had not begun to heal).

definitions.definition: ready to be used or ready to provide a service
examples: The supermarket is open till 8.00 p.m.
The road is open now, but it is often blocked by snow in the winter.
The new hospital was declared open by the mayor.

definitions.definition: not closed in or covered
examples: It's not a good idea to camp in the middle of an open field (= one which is not covered with trees, bushes, etc.).
Suddenly we had left the city behind and were out in open country.
The survivors were adrift on the open sea (= far from land).

definitions.definition: If a computer document or program is open, it is ready to be read or used
examples: Make sure you have both files open at the same time.

definitions.definition: available; not limited
examples: There are still several possibilities open to you.
The competition is open to anyone over the age of 16.
Is the library open to the general public?
Their whole attitude to these negotiations is open to criticism (= can be criticized).
I'd like to think I'm open to (= willing to consider) any reasonable suggestion.
An accident would lay the whole issue of safety open (= cause it to be considered).

definitions.definition: not secret
examples: There has been open hostility between them ever since they had that argument last summer.

definitions.definition: honest and not trying to keep things secret
examples: He's very open about his weaknesses.
I wish you'd be more open with me, and tell me what you're feeling.
She has an honest, open face.

definitions.definition: not decided or certain
examples: We don't have to make a firm decision yet. Let's leave it open.
We can leave our offer open for another week, but we have to have your decision by then.
I want to keep my options open, so I'm not committing myself yet.

definitions.definition: An open question can be answered in many different ways, and not with just "yes" or "no" or by choosing one of a number of fixed answers
examples: Ask an open question along the lines of "What do you think/feel about this?"
The interviewer should ask open and probing questions.

definitions.definition: to (cause to) begin
examples: I would like to open my talk by giving a brief background to the subject.
I'm going to open an account with another bank.
The Olympic Games open tomorrow.
A new radio station is due to open (up) next month.
The film opens (= will be shown for the first time) in New York and Los Angeles next week.

definitions.definition: to move something to a position that is not closed, or to make something change to a position that is not closed
examples: Could you open the window, please?
You can open your eyes now - here's your present.
The flowers open (out) in the morning but close again in the afternoon.
From the kitchen there is a door that opens (out) into the garden.
That door opens (out) onto the patio.
"Open up (= open the door) - it's the police!" shouted the police officer, banging on the door.

definitions.definition: to remove or separate part of a container or parcel so that you can see or use what it contains
examples: Don't open a new bottle just for me.
I couldn't wait to open the letter.

definitions.definition: If a shop or office opens at a particular time of day, it starts to do business at that time
examples: The coffee shop opens at ten o'clock.
He opens (up) his coffee shop at ten o'clock.

definitions.definition: If someone, usually someone important, opens a building, event, etc., they officially say that it is ready to be used or to start operating
examples: The new hospital will be officially opened by the mayor on Tuesday.

definitions.definition: to make something available
examples: This research opens (up) the possibility of being able to find a cure for the disease.
The country is planning to open (up) its economy to foreign investment.

definitions.definition: If you open a computer document or program, you make it ready to read or use
examples: To open a new document, click "File" and then click "New".
Click the desktop icon to open the Cambridge Advanced Learner's Dictionary.

definitions.definition: somewhere outside, rather than in a building
examples: It's good to be (out) in the open after being cooped up in an office all day.

definitions.definition: to tell people information that was secret

definitions.definition: to do something in a way that is too extreme
examples: After a heart attack you have to be careful not to overdo it/things (= you have to work and live calmly).

definitions.definition: to take more money out of your bank account than the account contains
examples: I overdrew my account by £20.

definitions.definition: too eager

definitions.definition: to guess an amount that is too high or a size that is too big
examples: I overestimated and there was a lot of food left over after the party.
Most of us overestimate how much time and energy we have.
According to the survey, agents frequently overestimate the size of rooms.

definitions.definition: to think that something is better or more important than it really is
examples: We cannot overestimate the importance of this work.
The benefits of nuclear technology, she said, had been grossly overestimated.

definitions.definition: to think that someone is stronger, or more skilful, intelligent, or determined than they really are
examples: They were forced to the conclusion that they had overestimated him.

definitions.definition: a guess that is too high in amount
examples: The shortfall was caused by an overestimate of revenue last year.

definitions.definition: too happy and enthusiastic

definitions.definition: too active
examples: The drug is thought to reduce pain by calming overexcited nerve cells.

definitions.definition: to make someone or something experience something too much, or for too long, risking harm
examples: He is another employee who was regularly overexposed to lead, arsenic, and cadmium in his 26 years at the plant.

definitions.definition: to give too much attention to someone or something in newspapers, on television, on social media, etc.
examples: His parents and his team protected him from being overexposed, keeping him from doing interviews and turning down endorsement deals.

definitions.definition: to give too much light to a piece of photographic film when taking a photograph
examples: Unfortunately the light was too bright and my pictures were all overexposed.
Since my camera allows me to preset exposure above and below the automatic reading, I typically overexpose my shots by one f-stop.

examples: A child's development can be irreversibly stunted by overexposure to lead.

definitions.definition: the fact of a person, event, or information appearing or being discussed too much in newspapers, on the television, on social media, etc.
examples: He worries about overexposure and how his friends will react to his new level of fame.
The show died a sudden death from overexposure after ABC decided to air it four nights a week.

definitions.definition: the fact of a piece of film being in the light for too long when a photograph is being taken
examples: He rescued the photo from overexposure and processed it in his darkroom.
The photographs were partially bleached by overexposure.

definitions.definition: to spend too much money, or borrow more money than you can pay back
examples: It is a sad fact that many people overextended their mortgage commitments and now face difficulties.
Borrowers overextend themselves in refinancing to cover unforeseen expenses.

definitions.definition: to try to do more work or other activities than you can manage
examples: The fear of failure can lead young people to overextend themselves.
He rejects opportunities that he thinks would overextend the firm's resources.

definitions.definition: having spent too much money, or borrowed more money than you can pay back
examples: Typically, it is people in their late 30s with mortgage commitments who find themselves overextended.
He predicted that the nation's overextended banks would start to fail.

definitions.definition: an area of very rough water caused by a raised area in the ground under the water, or by the meeting of two currents (= movements of water in a particular direction)
examples: The water can throw up great overfalls, which small boats should try to avoid.

definitions.definition: having been given too much food
examples: An overfed horse which has too little exercise is likely to be over-excited.
They are not malnourished, but they are certainly not overfed.

definitions.definition: to give a person or animal too much food
examples: Many owners accidentally overfeed their pets.
It's difficult to overfeed a breast-fed baby.

definitions.definition: the practice of giving a person or animal too much food
examples: Many parents mistake overfeeding for love.
Even slight overfeeding can lead to unpleasant digestive outcomes.

definitions.definition: catching too many fish in an area of the sea, so that there are not many fish left there
examples: low fish stocks caused by overfishing

definitions.definition: an occasion when an aircraft flies over a place
examples: Residents campaigned to reduce airline overflights in the area.
An overflight after the hurricane revealed a shocking level of damage.

definitions.definition: When a liquid overflows, it flows over the edges of a container, etc. because there is too much of it
examples: The milk overflowed when I poured it into the jug.
Because of heavy rain, the river may overflow its banks.

definitions.definition: If a container or a place overflows, whatever is inside it starts coming out because it is too full
examples: Oh no, the sink is overflowing all over the floor.
The bin was overflowing with rubbish.

definitions.definition: When a place overflows, or people or things overflow from somewhere, some people or things have to come out because it cannot contain them all
examples: The bar was so full that people were overflowing into/onto the street.
His room is overflowing with books.
The train was full to overflowing (= so full that there was not space for any more passengers).

definitions.definition: so that water or another substance is almost coming over the top
examples: Someone had filled the sink to overflowing.
The sink was full to overflowing.

definitions.definition: If you overflow with thoughts or feelings, you express them strongly
examples: They were overflowing with emotion at the birth of their baby.
Suddenly, her anger overflowed.

definitions.definition: an amount of liquid or number of people that cannot fit in a space
examples: I put a bucket underneath to catch the overflow from the water tank.
We can't cope with this overflow of patients from the other hospitals.

definitions.definition: a pipe that carries away water that is not needed

examples: Another problem is erosion of stream banks caused by overgrazing.

examples: The field is overgrown with weeds.

definitions.definition: used to describe something that has grown too large
examples: Cottonwood has become an overgrown town with inadequate facilities.

definitions.definition: used to describe an adult who behaves like a child
examples: They're acting like overgrown kids.

definitions.definition: (especially of a throw) made with the arm moving above the shoulder
examples: an overhand throw/serve

definitions.definition: to stick out over something at a lower level
examples: Several large trees overhang the path.

definitions.definition: to have a negative effect on a situation
examples: Overhanging the controversy is the question of how much the administration knew about the arms deal.

definitions.definition: the part of a rock or roof that sticks out over something below
examples: The church is unsafe because it was built on an overhang.

definitions.definition: something that has a negative effect on a situation
examples: Prices are unlikely to increase while there is an overhang of 40,000 unsold new houses.

definitions.definition: to repair an engine, machine, etc. so that every part of it works as it should
examples: I got the engine overhauled.

definitions.definition: to completely change a system so that it works more effectively
examples: The government has recently overhauled the healthcare system.
The school chose to overhaul its curriculum by equipping classrooms with new technology.

definitions.definition: to completely change something, especially by getting rid of some people or things and replacing them with others
examples: Most women don't overhaul their wardrobes from season to season.

definitions.definition: the act of repairing an engine, machine, etc. so that every part works as it should

definitions.definition: past participle of override
(of a person who has the necessary authority) to decide against or refuse to accept a previous decision, an order, a person, etc.
examples: Every time I make a suggestion at work, my boss overrides me/it.
The president used his veto to override the committee's decision.

definitions.definition: to operate an automatic machine by hand
examples: He overrode the autopilot when he realized it was malfunctioning.

definitions.definition: to take control over something, especially in order to change the way it operates
examples: The pills are designed to override your body's own hormones.

definitions.definition: to be more important than something
examples: Parents' concern for their children's future often overrides all their other concerns.

definitions.definition: to travel on public transport further than your ticket allows you to
examples: There is a $20 penalty for passengers who travel without a ticket or override.

definitions.definition: a device that changes the control of a machine or system in special situations, especially from automatic to manual
examples: The heating system has a manual override.

definitions.definition: in American politics, an occasion when an elected group of people refuses to accept a decision made by an elected leader
examples: The vote fell short of the majority needed for an override of the Governor's veto.

examples: The sign over the door said "Exit".

examples: Put a clean cloth over the cakes while they cool.

definitions.definition: across from one side to the other, especially by going up and then down

definitions.definition: falling down from somewhere

definitions.definition: on the other side of
examples: There's a bar over the road we could go to.

definitions.definition: during something, or while doing something

definitions.definition: to feel physically or mentally better after an illness or an upsetting experience

definitions.definition: in control of or teaching someone or something

definitions.definition: using

a mixture of oxygen and acetylene (= a gas) that produces a hot bright flame, and that can be used for cutting metal
examples: an oxyacetylene lamp/torch

definitions.definition: a strong drug that reduces pain and is related to codeine
examples: oxycodone addiction

definitions.definition: a chemical element that is a gas with no smell or colour. Oxygen forms a large part of the air on earth, and is needed by animals and plants to live.
to add oxygen to something
examples: Fish tanks often have a pump which oxygenates the water.

to add oxygen to something
examples: Fish tanks often have a pump which oxygenates the water.

examples: Fish tanks often have a pump which oxygenates the water.

definitions.definition: present participle of oxygenate
to add oxygen to something
examples: Fish tanks often have a pump which oxygenates the water.

definitions.definition: the process of adding oxygen to something
examples: Oxygenation of the blood is a key function of the lungs.

definitions.definition: the bright red form of haemoglobin (= substance in red blood cells) that contains oxygen
the bright red form of hemoglobin (= substance in red blood cells) that contains oxygen
two words used together that have, or seem to have, opposite meanings
(of two words used together) having, or seeming to have, opposite meanings
examples: Cultural tourism is close to being an oxymoronic phrase.
It might sound oxymoronic but there is such a thing as an interesting utility.

definitions.definition: two words used together that have, or seem to have, opposite meanings
a hormone that helps with labour (= the process of pushing out a baby) and helps women and female mammals to produce milk
another spelling of oi
a Yiddish exclamation used when someone is upset, shocked, disappointed, worried, etc.
examples: When she speaks of her children, she rubs her face and sighs, "Oy."

definitions.definition: a large flat sea creature that lives in a shell, some types of which can be eaten either cooked or uncooked, and other types of which produce pearls (= small round white precious stones)
one of two small pieces of chicken at the bottom of the backbone, which taste particularly good
examples: Chicken oysters are those juicy little chicken pieces hidden away between the leg and the backbone.
